frozen_components: hard verifier (SymPy symbolic checker for indefinite integral)
soft verifier (LLM judge with hard-coded filter rules)
architecture.summary: VHG is a three-party self-play framework for generating hard mathematical problems. A setter LLM generates problem-reference pairs; a verifier (hard symbolic or soft LLM-based) gates validity by checking correctness; a solver LLM provides difficulty feedback via pass-rate estimation. The setter's reward is conditioned on verifier acceptance: R(p,r) = (1 - accuracy) if verifier accepts (p,r), else 0. Only verifier-accepted pairs flow to solver training and challenge pools, preventing reward hacking through invalid hard problems.
trained_components: setter (generates problem-reference pairs via GRPO with verifier-gated reward)
solver (learns from verifier-accepted generated pairs via standard task-correctness RL)

biblio.title: Verifier-Backed Hard Problem Generation for Mathematical Reasoning

claims.statement: VHG raises Pass@1 on indefinite integral from 28.8% (Qwen3-4B-Base) to 45.4% (VHG with hard verifier) on Competition, from 52.5% to 69.4% on Qualifier, and from 43.3% to 64.7% on Integral Stress Test
On general math benchmarks, VHG raises overall pass@1 accuracy from 56.8% to 69.0% across MATH, AMC, Minerva, Olympiad, and AIME24-26

claims.dataset: AntiderivBench, Integral Stress Test, MATH, GSM8K, AMC, Minerva, Olympiad, AIME
claims.statement: VHG substantially outperforms all baseline methods (including R-Zero, vanilla GRPO) by a clear margin on both indefinite integral and general math tasks
On indefinite integral, VHG-generated problems verifier-accepted and zero pass-rate remain challenging for larger models: Qwen3-32B achieves Pass@1 of 46.99% and Pass@8 of 85.99%
On general math, VHG-generated zero pass-rate problems show Pass@1 of 41.31% and Pass@8 of 70.71% for Qwen3-32B (8× larger than setter/solver)

Setter learning exhibits a two-phase pattern: validity is learned first (reference-valid rate rises from 30.6% to 65.2% by step 50), then difficulty improves (solver pass rate among valid samples falls to 17.6% by step 200)
R-Zero does not outperform vanilla GRPO training on existing datasets after three iterations of self-play on indefinite integral, indicating importance of hard verification in self-play

VHG maintains higher validity rates than R-Zero across difficulty bins; consensus-based reward (R-Zero) shifts toward easy-to-intermediate problems with no retained candidates below certain accuracy threshold, while VHG generates harder problems with higher validity for hardest problems
